coredata.dc:title: Natural chitosan-based carbon dots as an eco-friendly and effective corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in HCl solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhen, Deshuai
Zhang, Shaoqi
Zhang, Xinyu
Zhang, Hongjian
Wang, Jue
Chen, Bo
Liu, Yali
Luo, Xiaohu
coredata.dc:description: Polysaccharide chitosan and L-histidine were applied to synthesize chitosan-based carbon dots (CA-CDs) by a simple laser ablation method. After characterization of the CA-CDs by FT-IR, UV–vis, Raman, XRD, TEM, and XPS, the CA-CDs were introduced as an eco-friendly and high-performance corrosion inhibitor for mild steel (MS) in 1.0 M HCl solution. The inhibition action and mechanism of CA-CDs were determined by weight loss and electrochemical measurements, in combination with SEM, AFM, and XPS. The results show that CA-CDs as mixed-type inhibitors could effectively weaken the corrosion of MS in 1.0 M HCl solution, and their maximum inhibition efficiency reaches 97.4 % at 40 mg L−1. The adsorption behavior of CA-CDs well obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm containing both chemisorption and physisorption. The chemisorption mainly results from the multiple adsorption sites in the CA-CDs, and the physical adsorption is due to the blocking and barrier effect of CA-CD nanoparticles. Both adsorption behaviors were proposed to elucidate the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CA-CDs.

Chitosan
Chemisorption
Electrochemical measurement

head.ce:title: Natural chitosan-based carbon dots as an eco-friendly and effective corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in HCl solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polysaccharide chitosan and L-histidine were applied to synthesize chitosan-based carbon dots (CA-CDs) by a simple laser ablation method. After characterization of the CA-CDs by FT-IR, UV–vis, Raman, XRD, TEM, and XPS, the CA-CDs were introduced as an eco-friendly and high-performance corrosion inhibitor for mild steel (MS) in 1.0 M HCl solution. The inhibition action and mechanism of CA-CDs were determined by weight loss and electrochemical measurements, in combination with SEM, AFM, and XPS. The results show that CA-CDs as mixed-type inhibitors could effectively weaken the corrosion of MS in 1.0 M HCl solution, and their maximum inhibition efficiency reaches 97.4 % at 40 mg L−1. The adsorption behavior of CA-CDs well obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm containing both chemisorption and physisorption. The chemisorption mainly results from the multiple adsorption sites in the CA-CDs, and the physical adsorption is due to the blocking and barrier effect of CA-CD nanoparticles. Both adsorption behaviors were proposed to elucidate the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CA-CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel (MS) plays an important role in the various industries of modern society [1–5] due to its attractive merits, such as good toughness, excellent chemical stability, easy preparation, and low price. At the same time, the corrosion of MS is very widespread in the natural environment, often leading to serious damage [4,5]. Corrosion inhibitors, as a common and efficient technology, have been widely applied to control the corrosion rate of MS because of their low cost, simplicity, and high efficiency [6–10]. However, some organic inhibitors (i.e., methylbenzotriazole chloride, methylbenzotriazole, and 5-chloro-2H-benzotriazole) not only are hard to biodegrade but also have some characteristics of expensive raw materials, high cost, and complicated synthesis steps [11–14]. These defects seriously limit the further application of organic inhibitors. Thus, it is indispensable to develop a sustainable and eco-friendly inhibitor with low-cost raw materials and a simple synthesis process.

Polysaccharides derived from natural products are considered potential natural inhibitors because of their rich active sites interacting with metal ions in solution [15,16]. Chitosan (CA) is a typical polysaccharide derived from the deacetylation of chitin, which is widely present in fungal cell walls and crab and shrimp shells. Because of its chemically stable, biodegradable, and nontoxic properties, CA and its derivatives have been widely applied in the medicine, food, and textile industries. Meanwhile, some reports have revealed that CA is a green corrosion inhibitor for mild steel and aluminum alloys in corrosive solutions due to its rich hydroxyl and amino groups [17,18]. However, their inhibitive performance is relatively low. Therefore, to enhance its corrosion inhibition efficiency, an increasing number of researchers have developed many methods (i.e., esterification, chemical grafting, and thiosemicarbazide modification) to chemically modify CA by introducing functional groups with abundant active sites [19]. For example, Cui et al. developed a two-step method to fabricate two different CA derivatives by the introduction of benzaldehyde and propionaldehyde. Two synthesized CA derivatives as mixed-type inhibitors have high inhibition performance with >80 % inhibitory efficiency at a concentration of 100 mg L−1 [20]. Zhang reported two different CA derivatives as green and highly efficient inhibitors of Q325 in corrosive solution. The corrosion inhibition efficiencies of the two CA derivatives are much larger than that of CA [21]. After modification, CA derivatives have improved inhibition performance, but the whole process, especially for chemical modification, exhibits some defects, such as complex synthesis steps, time consumption, and lack of environmental friendliness. More importantly, these CA derivatives still exhibit relatively poor inhibition performance. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and effective method to enhance the inhibitive performance of CA.

On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanomaterials, have undergone significant development and have been widely applied in various fields, including chemical analysis [23], biomedicine [24], sensors [25], and photocatalysts [26], which is attributable to their environmental friendliness, sustainability, low cost, and nontoxicity. To date, CDs, as green corrosion inhibitors, have attracted much attention in the corrosive protective field. CDs could form a protective layer on the protective metal surface through physical and chemical adsorption. For example, Zhu et al. used 4-amino salicylic acid (4-ASA) and L-histidine (L-His) as precursors to synthesize environmentally friendly and efficient N-doped carbon dots by a solvothermal method, and its anticorrosion efficiency reached 93 % at 50 mg L−1 [27]. The high anticorrosion efficiency is because the rich heteroatoms provide lone-pair electrons to bond with unoccupied orbitals of Fe atoms, resulting in the formation of an efficient protective film at the surface of the protective metal [27]. Wang et al. prepared three novel N-doped carbon dots through pyrolysis of ammonium citrate at 180 °C for different reaction times [6–28]. The authors studied the effect of the reaction times on the inhibition efficiency of carbon dots [28]. The inhibition efficiency of three carbon dots exceeded 90 % at 200 mg L-1, and the highest inhibitive efficiency was found for the carbon dots prepared at a reaction time of 2 h [28]. Ren et al. comparatively researched the anticorrosion behaviors of pure epoxy coatings and CD/epoxy composite coatings [29]. The results suggested that the introduction of CDs could significantly improve the anticorrosion performance of CD/epoxy composite coatings [29]. These reported results indicate that CDs could exhibit good inhibition efficiency.

Based on the above analyses, a simple and effective laser ablation method was developed to synthesize novel chitosan-based carbon dots (CA-CDs) with excellent corrosion inhibition performance by using CA and L-histidine as the raw materials. The physical properties of the CA-CDs were well determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–vis, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Subsequently, the corrosion inhibition performance of CA-CDs for mild steel (MS) in 1.0 M HCl was determined in detail. The maximum corrosion inhibition performance of CA-CDs is up to 97.4 %. Moreover, the adsorption dynamics and corrosion inhibition mechanism were explored. This work provides some interesting views to exploit eco-friendly and highly efficient corrosion inhibitors.

Chitosan (CA, 95 % of deacetylation degree, 120 mPa·s of viscosity, approximately 120,000 of molecular weight), L-histidine (C6H9N3O2), and other reagents were supplied by Sigma Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fig. 1(a) exhibits the synthesis processes of CA-CDs. CA-CDs were obtained through the simple method of pulsed laser ablation. Typically, 200 mg of CA was added to 100 mL of deionized H2O, and then 400 mg of L-histidine was gradually added to the above solution. The mixture solution was transferred to a 200 mL autoclave and placed in the pulsed laser. A nanosecond pulsed Nd:YAG laser (Dawa-350, Beamtech) was used. The critical parameters were set up, including the pulse width (18 ns), repetition frequency (50 Hz), wavelength (1680 nm), and single-pulse energy (220 mJ). The reaction was carried out for 1.0 h. After that, the reaction was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the as-prepared sample was washed with deionized water several times and then dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for 8 h. The final product was called CA-1 CD. It is noted that high-temperature and high-pressure conditions could be formed during the reaction process, so the selected autoclave must withstand these special conditions.
For comparison, the control CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD samples with 2.0 h and 3.0 h reaction times were obtained by the method above, respectively.

In this work, MS was chosen as the studied electrode. Table S1 lists the compositions of the MS. One side of the studied MS with a size of 1 cm × 1 cm × 0.5 cm was sealed by epoxy resin. The other side was polished with 150–1200 mesh sandpaper initially and then cleaned with deionized H2O and ethanol. After that, the pretreated working electrode was exposed to the electrolyte. In this work, a 1.0 M HCl solution without and with CA-CDs was used as the electrolyte.

The studied specimens were first soaked in a beaker containing 1.0 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of CA-CDs for different immersion times. After immersion, the specimens were removed and washed with distilled water and ethanol. After drying at 25 °C in an oven, the specimens were accurately weighed. To obtain good reproducibility, each experiment was repeated three times. The corrosion rate (C R) according to the weight loss can be calculated [30,31]:
(1)
C R
=
Δm
s × Δt
where Δt acts as the immersion time (h) and Δm reflects the average weight loss. S is the total area of the sample.

Electrochemical tests were studied in an electrochemical cell, in which there are three electrodes, including a graphite electrode with 3 mm in diameter as a counter electrode, a saturated calomel (Hg/Hg2Cl2, SCE) as a reference electrode, and a working electrode prepared by the studied MS. The frequency was set at 0.01 Hz - 100 kHz with a potential amplitude of ±10 mV during the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement. Polarization plots were obtained in a potential range from −250 mV to +250 mV at a 0.5 mV s−1 interval. The scanning vibrating electrode technique (SVET) was carried out to characterize the local corrosion of the samples. Some important parameters, including scanning vibration amplitude (2 μm), frequency (80 Hz), and scanning point (10 × 10), should be set. The EIS data were further fitted by Zsimp win demo version software. To ensure the authenticity of the data, each measurement was performed on three identical samples, and the mean values were recorded.

Fig. 1 shows a simple laser ablation method to synthesize a novel and effective chitosan-based CA-CD corrosion inhibitor by using polysaccharide chitosan and L-histidine. Our reports have demonstrated that when the laser-target interaction was introduced into the solution, it caused a strong heat-induced and/or photothermal effect in the local solution, which was beneficial for the formation or reshaping of nanomaterials [32,33]. In this work, CA and L-histidine and water were the reactants and the solvent, respectively. When the water was irradiated by a focused nanosecond pulsed laser, a plasma state was swiftly heated, leading to a significant increase in temperature to promote the formation of high-temperature and high-pressure conditions. Following that, the CA and L-histidine rapidly reacted to create the CA-CDs. The synthesized CA-CDs are displayed in Fig. S1. Compared with conventional methods (e.g., hydrothermal method, solvothermal method), it exhibits some obvious advantages, such as time savings, simple steps, and eco-friendliness.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM images and grain sizes of CA-CDs samples prepared under different reaction times. As displayed in Fig. 2(a) and Fig. 2(c), the CA-1 CD and CA-2 CD are homogeneously dispersed without agglomeration. The CA-1 CD exhibits a much smaller size located at 4–10 nm (Fig. 2(b)) than that of the CA-2 CD (Fig. 2(d)). However, with the reaction time increasing to 3 h, CA-3 CD exhibits serious agglomerations (Fig. 2(e)). The average sizes of the CA-3 CD increase to 10–80 nm (Fig. 2(f)). Generally, when the laser-target interaction was introduced into the solution, a high-temperature and high-pressure condition was formed. As the reaction time increased, the crystalline grains agglomerated under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions [33]. Thus, this could explain why the CA-3 CD exhibits a significant increase in the average size with increasing time. Agglomeration often leads to a decrease in the water-soluble property of CDs, which is verified by Fig. S2. It is found that there are some sediments in an aqueous solution of CA-3 CD (Fig. S2(c)) compared with CA-1 CD (Fig. S2(a)) and CA-2 CD (Fig. S2(b)).

To obtain the characteristic adsorption bands of the samples, FT-IR measurements were carried out, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S3. Two significant adsorption bands were located at approximately 3489 and 3450 cm−1, which were attributed to the NH and OH stretching vibrations, respectively [34,35]. The adsorption bands at ∼3061 and 2912 cm−1 should be attributed to the CH stretching from -CH3 and -CH2- groups [36], respectively. A weak absorption band at approximately 1787 cm−1 should be attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group [37]. The adsorption bands at 1659 and 1432 cm−1 correspond to an amide carbonyl stretching mode, which is considered an amide I vibrational stretch and CC stretching [38], respectively. A poor adsorption band at approximately 1315 cm−1 should be attributed to the CN stretching vibration [6], while the characteristic peak of the CO stretching vibration is at 1019 cm−1. These findings reveal the successful reaction between CA and L-histidine by the laser ablation method.

The UV–vis spectra, Raman spectra, and XRD patterns are displayed in Fig. 4 and Fig. S4. In the case of UV–vis spectra (Fig. 4(a)), CA-CDs show two characteristic adsorption peaks compared to CA and L-histidine, in which a strong peak located at approximately 203 nm should be attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of sp2 domains, and the other small adsorption band observed at approximately 295 nm corresponds to the n-π* electronic transition of CN bonds. It should be noted that compared to the CA-1 CD, the adsorption band of the n-π* electronic transition for CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD slightly shifts to the positive position. Previous results reported that the increased size of CDs could lead to a redshift of the n-π* electronic transition [23,26]. The XRD patterns of CA-2 CDs (Fig. S4)) show a characteristic peak of a graphite structure located at 2θ = 11.05°, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of graphite. Additionally, in the Raman spectra of CA-CDs (Fig. 4(b)), two obvious adsorption peaks are observed, located at approximately 1354.6 and 1578.7 cm−1. Generally, the peaks at 1354.6 and 1578.7 cm−1 are assigned to the D-band and G-band of graphite, respectively. The D-band and G-band arise from the first-order scattering of the E1g photon of the sp2 C atom and the breathing mode of point photons of A1g symmetry [39,40], respectively. This suggests that CA-CDs have sp2-hybridized and sp3-hybridized carbons. Additionally, the I D/I G (the relative intensity of the D band to the G band) values of CA-1, CA-2, and CA-3 CDs are 1.351, 1.321 and 1.303, respectively. This implies that with the increase in reaction time, the content of sp2-hybridized carbon rises.

To obtain the elemental valence information, the XPS spectra of the CA-CDs were determined as displayed in Fig. 5 . C, N, and O elements were detected in Fig. 5(a). As summarized in Table S2, we found that with the reaction time, the contents of C and N slowly decreased and increased, respectively, while the O content was relatively stable. This was due to the N atoms in the place of C atoms in the CA-CDs with the reaction time. To further analyze the chemical states of C 1 s and N 1 s, C and N fine spectra were fitted by Gaussian fitting software. The C 1 s spectrum was fitted with three peaks at approximately 284.6, 285.7, and 288.4 eV corresponding to CC, C=N/C-N, and CO bonds (Fig. S5), respectively. This is consistent with the analysis of the above FT-IR and UV–vis spectra. Meanwhile, three peaks are observed in the N 1 s spectra of CA-CDs, where 393.3, 399.6 eV, and 400.7 eV correspond to pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N [33] (Fig. 5(b-c)), respectively. Furthermore, the contents of different states of N are summarized in Table S3. It is found that the graphitic N content increases with the reaction time. For example, the graphitic N contents of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD are (16.32 ± 0.11)%, (32.43 ± 0.25)%, and (12.53 ± 0.06)%, respectively, which agrees with the above Raman results.

A weight loss test is often carried out to study the C R of corrosion inhibitors due to its simple and valid features. In this work, the C R results of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD obtained by the weight loss measurement are shown in Fig. 6 . When the CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD were added to the solution, the C R values of the MS sharply decreased initially and then remained stable. This suggests that CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD could inhibit the corrosion of MS in 1.0 M HCl solution, playing a corrosion inhibitor role. Meanwhile, it should be noted that compared to the CA-1 CD and CA-3 CD, the CA-2 CD shows a more significant reduction in the C R. For example, the minimum C R value of the CA-2 CD decreases to only 0.110 mg cm−2 h−1 at a concentration of 40 mg L−1, much lower than that of the CA-1 CD (0.663 mg cm−2 h−1) and CA-3 CD (0.726 mg cm−2 h−1). This implies that the CA-2 CD shows the largest corrosion inhibition among them, while the CR of the CA-1 CD is higher than that of the CA-3 CD. The successful protection action for the CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD is due to the formation of the protection film at the surface of MS, which could inhibit the corrosion of MS [41,42].

Fig. 7 shows the open circuit potential (E (OCP))-time curves of the samples. In the case of the blank solution, the E (OCP) slowly rises initially and then remains at approximately −0.386 V/SCE with the immersion time. This is caused by corrosion at the surface of the MS [23]. When the CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD were added to the 1.0 M HCl solution, they showed a different change in the E (OCP) compared to that in the blank solution with the immersion time, in which their E (OCP) values were reduced with the immersion time. This reflects the corrosion inhibition behaviors of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD, which should be attributable to the adsorption of CA-CD molecules on the MS surface, which could make the interface of the working electrode have a high charge transfer resistance [23–25]. The E (OCP) of CA-2 CD shows a more negative reduction than that of CA-1 CD and CA-3 CD, suggesting that it has the best corrosion inhibition performance among them [23,24]. After ∼350 min of immersion time, the E (OCP) values of the CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD stabilize at ∼ −0.416, −0.442 V/SCE, and -0.409 V/SCE, respectively.

In this work, potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements were carried out to reveal the influence of the addition of CA-CDs on the corrosion behaviors of MS in 1.0 M HCl solution. Fig. 8 shows that the addition of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD could cause a negative shift in the corrosion potential (E corr) and substantially reduce the corrosion current density (i corr) of the anode and cathode compared with the blank occasion. The shapes of the PPD curves of CA-CDs exhibit no obvious differences relative to the blank solution, suggesting that the CA-CDs only inhibit the corrosion process of MS by slowing down the kinetics of the electrode reaction rather than changing the reaction mechanism [43–45]. The degree of negative shift of E corr of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD is 14, 36, and 17 mV, respectively, which is <85 mV. This indicates that CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD and CA-3 CD are considered mix-type inhibitors. In addition, the lines in the cathodic area are relatively separate, suggesting that the inhibitive effect of CA-CDs on the hydrogen evolution reaction at the cathode is predominant.

Similarly, a smaller i corr could reflect a higher corrosion inhibition activity. Compared to the CA-1 CD (26.1 ± 0.2 μA cm−2) and CA-3 CD (36.9 ± 0.2 μA cm−2), the CA-2 CD had a lower i corr (8.6 ± 0.1 μA cm−2) in the presence of 40 mg L−1, further indicating the stronger protective performance of the CA-2 CD. The inhibition efficiency (η
i ) was determined based on the following equation [46,47]:
(2)
η i
%
=
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
inh
i
corr
0
× 100 %

where i corr(0) and i corr(inh) are the corrosion current densities for MS in the absence and presence of CA-CDs. The results listed in Table 1 show that the η
i values of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD are 91.8 %, 97.3 %, and 88.4 %, respectively. η
i for CA-2 CD is the highest among them, and the C R of CA-1 CD is larger than that of CA-3 CD. Additionally, the variations in the Tafel anodic slope (β a) and Tafel cathodic slope (β c) for CA-CDs further suggest that the CA-CDs act as mixed-type inhibitors [48–50], which could affect both the anodic dissolution of MS and the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction [51,52].

To further understand the corrosion behaviors at the interface between MS and a solution containing HCl, EIS measurements were conducted on the working electrode. The obtained Nyquist diagrams of MS immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of CA-CDs are shown in Fig. 9 . It is found that the whole Nyquist plots from both uninhibited and inhibited solutions have an irregular semicircle where the center under the imaginary axis is suppressed, indicating that nonideal electrochemical reactions happen on the MS surface [53,54]. With the addition of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD, their Nyquist plots showed an increased diameter compared with the blank one, which suggests that the charge transfer resistance at the interface of the MS and solution is enhanced, resulting from the enhancement of the protective film at the interface between the MS and solution [55,56]. Obviously, all the Nyquist plots have only one single capacitive semicircle in the whole frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 KHz, which means that the electrochemical reactions at the interfaces between CA-CDs and MS could be reflected through the typical model of the double-layer capacitance (C dl). At the same time, the presence of a single loop indicates the participation of one time constant in the EIS spectra. The curves of the phase angle for MS are shown in Fig. 9(d). It is observed that all the phase angle plots have only one peak, which further suggests that only one time constant appears in the corrosion process.

Randle's circuit, as shown in Fig. S6, containing solution resistance (R s), constant phase element (CPE), and charge-transfer resistance (R ct), was applied to fit the Nyquist plots to obtain the corrosion parameters. The modulus of the CPE can be computed as follows [54,55]:
(3)
Z CPE
=
1

αω
n
where Y
0 is the amplitude of the CPE and n is the phase shift in the range from −1 to 1, which can confirm the degree of the dispersion index. When n is −1, the CPE can be seen as an inductor. For n = 0 or 1, the CPE acts as a pure resistance or a pure capacitor. α is the imaginary root, and ω is the angular frequency (rad s−1). In addition, the following equation could determine the double-layer capacitance (C dl) value and the inhibition efficiency (η
R %) [56–58]:
(4)

(5)

where f max is the frequency at which the imaginary component of the interfacial impedance is at a maximum, and R (inh) and R (0) act as the R ct values for MS in 1.0 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of CA-CDs, respectively. After fitting, the obtained corrosion parameters are listed in Table 2 . Table 2 shows that all the samples have a very close Rs value located between 3.7 and 3.9 Ω cm2. As the CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD are added to the corrosive solution, the R ct value significantly rises compared with the blank value. For instance, R ct values for CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD are 440.9 ± 0.1, 1492.3 ± 0.2, and 340.4 ± 0.2 Ω cm2, respectively, while the R ct value for the blank solution is only 38.8 Ω cm2. The increased R ct for CA-CDs should be due to the added CA-CDs adsorbing on the MS surface to form a protective film, thus seriously hindering the charge transfer at the interface between the MS and solution. Meanwhile, it is seen that the C dl value is seriously reduced by the introduction of CA-CDs. The reduced C dl value is mainly attributed to the reduction in the local dielectric constant or the increase in the thickness of the double-layer capacitor [59,60]. With the formation of a protective film on the MS surface by the adsorbed CA-CDs replacing the preadsorbed H2O molecules, the active sites were covered. As expected, CA-2 CD had the largest inhibition efficiency, and its η
R % was up to 97.4 % at a concentration of 40 mg L-1. CA-1 CD and CA-3 CD have ηR% values of 91.2 % and 88.6 %, respectively.

SVET, as an in situ technology, could provide accurate electrochemical information. In this work, SVET was conducted to confirm the local corrosion current of MS in 1.0 M HCl solution without and with CA-1 CD (40 mg L−1), CA-2 CD (40 mg L−1), and CA-3 CD (40 mg L−1). It can be seen in Fig. 10(a) that a high positive current density is observed in the studied region, suggesting that there is serious corrosion on the MS surface. With the introduction of CA-1 CD, CA-2 CD, and CA-3 CD in the solution, it was found that the current density was evidently reduced in the studied region (Fig. 10(b-d)). This could reflect the significant weakening of the corrosion process on the MS surface by the CA-CDs. In addition, compared with the CA-3 CD (Fig. 10(b)) and CA-1 CD (Fig. 10(c)), the CA-2 CD has the smallest current density (Fig. 10(d)), further exhibiting the best protection activity.

The surface morphology of MS before and after CA-CDs was analyzed by SEM and AFM. Fig. 11 shows the morphology of MS samples immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution without (Fig. 11(a)) and with CA-1 CD (40 mg L−1) (Fig. 11(b)), CA-3 CD (40 mg L−1) (Fig. 11(c)), and CA-2 CD (40 mg L−1) (Fig. 11(d)) for 24 h at 25 °C. The rude surface and amount of corrosion products occurred on the MS surface (Fig. 11(a)), suggesting that MS is seriously corroded by aggressive mediums. After the addition of CA-CDs to the HCl solution, the surface of MS is less rough, and the corrosion products are greatly reduced (Fig. 11(b-d)), indicating that corrosion is evidently alleviated by CA-CDs. At the same time, a smoother surface is observed for MS in the CA-2 CD solution compared to the CA-1 CD and CA-3 CD, further directly revealing the best corrosion inhibition performance of CA-2 CD among them.

Three-dimensional (3D) AFM was carried out to further study the surface features, as shown in Fig. 12 . As shown in Fig. 12(a, c), the MS in the blank solution exhibits more disorderly pits and porosity, and its corresponding height profile is up to 300 nm, indicating severe corrosion. When the CA-2 CDs are added to the corrosive solution, the MS surface is relatively flat (Fig. 12(b)), and the corresponding height profile of the MS surface significantly decreases to only 80 nm (Fig. 12(d)). Meanwhile, the average roughness (Ra) of the MS surface decreases from 102.45 nm to 35.62 nm. Thus, the above result is in agreement with the SEM results, further confirming that CA-2 CDs can effectively inhibit the corrosion of MS in a corrosive solution.

Based on the above analyses, the CA-2 CD has the best inhibition performance, while the CA-3 CD exhibited the poorest anticorrosion performance. This suggests that as the reaction time increases, the inhibitive performance of CA-CDs initially increases and then decreases. This should be attributable to these main factors. One is that CA-3 CDs have the poorest water solubility due to agglomeration (Fig. S2), inevitably leading to a decrease in the number of inhibitors in the solution. More importantly, CA-2 CDs not only have the highest contents of N atoms (Table S2) but also exhibit rich pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N atoms (Table S3). N atoms, especially pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N atoms, have rich lone pair electrons, which could easily fill in the unoccupied 3D orbitals of Fe atoms [61,62], forming strong coordination between CA-CDs and Fe atoms from MS. In addition, graphitic N atoms cause the agglomeration of CDs on steel substrate surfaces, increasing the size of CA-3 CDs, thus significantly reducing their active adsorption sites. In addition, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CA-2 CDs reaches 97.4 % in a 1.0 M HCl solution containing 40 mg−1 L, which is higher than some recently reported inhibition efficiencies, as shown in Table S4. Next, we chose CA-2 CDs to further study their influencing factors and adsorption actions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of factors on the protection performance
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 shows the effect of the concentration of CA-2 CD on the protective performance, and the corresponding fitted EIS parameters are summarized in Table 3 . It can be seen in Fig. 13(a-b) that when the concentration of CA-2 CD rises, the diameter of Nyquist plots and the impedance modulus at low frequency of Bode plots of samples sharply increases in the beginning, but little by little the increase in the diameter of Nyquist plots could be ignored. This indicates that the increase in the concentration could enhance the protective performance of CA-2 CD to inhibit the corrosion of MS in the corrosive solution. Table 3 shows the changes in the inhibition efficiency of CA-2 CD for different immersion times. As expected, as the immersion time was prolonged, the inhibition efficiency showed a remarkable increase at first and then remained stable. For instance, with the concentration of CA-2 CD increasing from 0 to 40 mg L−1, the inhibition efficiency increased from 82.5 % to 97.4 %, increasing by 18 %. However, as the concentration further increased to 70 mg L−1, the inhibition efficiency was maintained at 97.5 %. This suggests that the critical micelle concentration is 40 mg L−1. Some reports revealed that when the added concentration is smaller than the critical micelle concentration, an increasing number of inhibitor molecules could adsorb at the surface of the metal, replacing H2O, thus leading to the enhancement of the protective layer on the MS surface [36,51]. However, as it reaches the critical concentration, the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the MS surface attains the saturation state. Therefore, this can explain why the inhibition efficiency cannot further increase with the concentration.

Fig. 14 shows the effect of immersion time on the corrosion inhibition performance of CA-2 CD, and the corresponding polarization parameters are listed in Table 4 . Table 4 shows that as the immersion time increases, E corr and i corr markedly increase and decrease, respectively. However, after 24 h of immersion, E corr and i corr remained stable. For example, they then remain relatively stable (Table 4). The E corr and i corr values for the CA-2 CD increase and decrease from −0.385 ± 0.004 V/SCE to −0.421 ± 0.008 V/SCE and from 318.5 ± 0.5 μA cm−2 to 8.6 ± 0.1 μA cm−2 after 24 h of immersion, respectively. However, as the immersion time further increases for the CA-2 CD, the E corr and i corr values remain at −0.420 ± 0.008 V/SCE and 8.5 ± 0.3 μA cm−2, respectively. Meanwhile, the inhibition efficiency of the CA-2 CD has a similar change. For example, when the immersion time increases to 24 h, its corrosion inhibition efficiency significantly increases. However, with the continuous increase in immersion time, the inhibition efficiency remains at approximately 97.3 %. This reflects that prolonging the immersion time does not infinitely enhance the inhibition efficiency of the inhibitor, which is due to the formation of the saturated adsorption status [63,64].

The temperature could not only affect the corrosion efficiency but also influence the stability of the corrosion inhibitor. In this work, the effect of temperature on the inhibition behavior of CA-2 CD was studied, and the results are shown in Table 5 . Table 5 shows that the C R of MS in 1.0 M HCl solution with and without CA-2 CD at different temperatures shows the same change trends. For example, with increasing temperature, the corrosion inhibition efficiency significantly decreases. This could be explained by the fact that because the adsorption/desorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface is a thermodynamic equilibrium process, desorption will occur at a relatively high temperature [64,65]. Therefore, a high temperature must cause a decrease in the corrosion inhibition efficiency.

To obtain the adsorption activation energy (E a) of CA-2 CD during the adsorption process, the Arrhenius equation was used as follows [28,63]:
(6)
In
C R
= −
E a
RT
+ λ
where λ is the Arrhenius preexponential factor and R is the gas constant. At the same time, T and C R are the absolute temperature and corrosion rate at different temperatures, respectively. Obviously, the linear regression of ln(C R) vs. 1/T could be used to obtain the E a value. Meanwhile, a high linear regression coefficient (R2 > 0.978) is observed (Fig. 15 ), suggesting the good feasibility of the kinetic model [66,67]. After calculation, the obtained E a values and other parameters are listed in Table S5. Table S5 shows that the E a value of the CA-2 CD at different temperatures is higher than that in the blank solution. The high energy barrier in the presence of CA-2 CD reveals that the electrochemical corrosion reaction is remarkably restrained.

To confirm the adsorption actions of the studied CA-2 CD on the MS surface, we studied the adsorption isotherm of CA-2 CD during the adsorption process. Three different adsorption theories, Langmuir, Temkin, and Frumkin adsorption isotherms, can elucidate the adsorption behaviors of inhibitors on the substrate surface [66–70]. Compared to the Temkin and Frumkin adsorption isotherms, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm has been more frequently applied to explain the adsorption analysis of inhibitors. It is noted that the Langmuir adsorption isotherm should comply with the energetic equivalent of every adsorption site and the monolayer inhibitor film formed on the surface. The best description of the adsorption action of CA-2 CD could be explained by the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, as given by Eq. [71]:
(7)
C inh
θ
=
1
K ads
+
C inh

In this equation, C inh represents the CA-2 CD concentration, and K ads is the equilibrium constant for the adsorption-desorption process. Meanwhile, the θ value is equal to the inhibition efficiency (η), reflecting the surface coverage of CA-2 CD. After the linear regression of C inh/θ vs. C inh, it is found in Fig. 16 that all regression coefficients (R2) exhibit large values (> 0.980). The results show a good linear relationship, which reflects that the adsorption action of CA-2 CD obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and that the CA-2 CDs form a monolayer film against corrosion. The K ads value obtained by the reciprocal of the intercept of the straight line decreases with increasing temperature (Table 6 ), which indicates that the CA-2 CD exhibits a relatively higher adsorption tendency on the MS surface at low temperatures. Meanwhile, the K ads value was applied to determine the standard Gibbs free energy (Δ
G ads 0
) by using the following Eq. [65]:
(8)
Δ
G ads 0
= − RT ln
55.5
K ads

in which T represents the studied absolute temperature and R is the molar gas constant (8.314 J/(mol·K)). 55.5 mol L−1 signifies the concentration of water. After the calculation, all the Δ
G ads 0
values are presented in Table 6. The Δ
G ads 0
value is negative, suggesting that the CA-2 CD could spontaneously adsorb over the MS surface. Recent reports demonstrated that the Δ
G ads 0
value is less than −40 kJ mol−1, which means that the type of adsorption of inhibitors on the substrate surface is chemisorption adsorption [68]. In contrast, if the adsorption obeys physisorption, the Δ
G ads 0
value will be higher than −20 kJ mol−1 [69,70]. In the case, the calculated Δ
G ads 0
values are between −34 and − 37 kJ mol−1 (Table 6), which means that the adsorption of CA-2 CD on the MS surface involves both physisorption and chemisorption, but compared to physisorption, the main adsorption should be chemisorption [72–74].

XPS spectroscopy was applied to study the chemical composition and bond state at the interface between the CA-2 CD and MS surface. The full XPS spectrum of the sample after immersion in 1.0 M HCl solution containing the CA-2 CD for 24 h is presented in Fig. 17(a). It can be seen that the O, C, N, and Fe elements were observed, which proves the presence of CA-2 CD adsorbed on the MS surface. To obtain the chemical bonding information of each element, the high-resolution XPS spectra of each studied element are shown in Fig. 17(b-e).

The Fe 2p3/2 spectrum was fitted into four peaks, where the peaks were distributed at 708.6, 710.3, 713.4, and 715.3 eV (Fig. 17(b)). The peak at 708.6 eV is attributed to the metal Fe. The binding energy located at 710.3 eV should be Fe oxides such as FeO/Fe-O. The peak situated at 712.4 should be due to the Fe-N x bond. The last peak at 715.3 eV should be attributable to Fe3+ (satellite) [67]. At the same time, a relatively small peak is at 723.2 eV. This peak is associated with Fe 2p1/2 [75].

In the case of the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 17(c)), there are three main peaks. The peaks at 284.7, 285.6, and 288.3 eV correspond to C-C/C=N, CN, and C=O/N-C=O [56], respectively, which should be derived from CA-2 CD.

For the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 17(d)), the O 1 s spectrum was split into five peaks. The binding energies of 529.4, 529.8, and 531.4 eV should be Fe2O3, FeO/O-Fe, and FeOOH, respectively, while the peaks at 530.8 and 532.1 eV are due to CO and CO bonds, respectively.

Finally, the N 1 s spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks (Fig. 17(e)), in which the binding energies of 398.5, 399.8 and 400.4 eV correspond to the NH, NC, and NC bonds, respectively. More importantly, the peak at 399.5 eV should be due to the NFe bond. Therefore, the chemisorption of CA-2 CD on the MS surface can be confirmed by the NFe and Fe-N x bonds, which result from the coordination bonds between N atoms in the CA-2 CD and Fe atoms.

Because of the smaller radius and strong penetration activity of chloride ions (Cl−), it is easy for Cl− to adsorb at the surface of MS to activate the Fe atom. Meanwhile, the O atom has a strong oxidizability. When the O2 is dissolved in the solution, it could easily oxidize the activated Fe to produce the Fe2+/Fe3+ species through the redox reaction. In addition, hydrogen ions (H+) obtained by the hydrolysis of Fe2+ could further promote the dissolution of Fe. Based on the synergistic effect of these factors, the corrosion of MS in the solution containing Cl− will accelerate.

Generally, physical/chemical adsorption and coordination reactions between the corrosion inhibitor and Fe atoms have been regarded as the most acceptable anticorrosion mechanism. Heteroatoms such as N and O atoms containing rich lone pair electrons could form coordinate-covalent bonds between the heteroatom and Fe atoms by filling the lone pair electrons into the unoccupied 3d-orbitals of Fe atoms. According to the FT-IR, UV–vis, Raman, and XPS analyses, CA-CDs have rich active groups, such as carboxyl, amido bond, hydroxyl, and other groups, which could act as active adsorption sites. The results from XPS spectra suggest the existence of FeN bonds, which could confirm the adsorption of some active groups on the MS surface by the coordination bonds to form a protective film, which could effectively prevent the corrosive medium from invading. Based on the Langmuir adsorption isotherm analysis, the Δvalue of CA-CDs ranges from −34 to −37 kJ mol−1, suggesting that the adsorption action of CA-CDs also contains physical adsorption. The physical adsorption should be due to the agglomeration and multilayer accumulation of graphite carbon rings in the CA-CDs on the MS surface. Meanwhile, when CA-CDs diffused to the bareness point of the MS surface and chemisorbed layer, the corrosive mediums were driven out of the MS/solution interface and displaced by CA-CDs through the existence of the weaker lateral repulsive force between them. Therefore, based on the above discussion, Fig. 18 shows the adsorption mechanism for CA-CDs.

In summary, XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis, XRD, Raman, TEM, and other investigations suggest that chitosan-based carbon dots (CA-CDs) were successfully fabricated by the developed laser ablation method with simple and time-saving features using polysaccharide chitosan and L-histidine. TEM and SPM measurements reveal that as the reaction time increases, the CA-CDs not only exhibit a large size but also agglomerate, which leads to a decrease in their water solubility. FT-IR and XPS analyses strongly prove that the N atoms exhibit different chemical states, such as pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N, in the CA-CDs. Additionally, the reaction time plays an important role in regulating the content and chemical states of N atoms. When the reaction time is 2 h, the CA-2 CDs not only have the largest content of N atoms but also contain the highest content of pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N. Electrochemical measurements and weight loss tests show that the prepared CA-CDs, as mixed-type inhibitors, exhibit outstanding corrosion inhibition performance for MS in a 1.0 M HCl solution, and the inhibition efficiency is >88.0 %. Compared to CA-1 CD and CA-3 CD, CA-2 CD has the largest inhibitory performance (∼ 97.4 %). The enhanced inhibition performance of CA-2 CD should mainly be due to their good water solubility and high N atom content, especially for pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N. In addition, the results from the Δ
G ads 0
value and XPS spectra confirm that the adsorption of CA-2 CD obeys the Langmuir isotherm on the MS surface, involving physisorption as well as chemisorption.
